questions.question:
Ms. X came out of a building through its front door to find her shadow due to the morning sun falling to her right side with the building to her back. From this, it can be inferred that the building is facing _________.

A. North  B. East  C. West  D. South

understand.plain: In the morning the sun rises in the East, so sunlight comes from the East and shadows fall to the West. Ms. X is standing outside facing away from the building (her back is to the building, so she faces away from it). Her shadow falls to her right. We need to determine which direction she is facing, and from that, which direction the front of the building faces.

keywords.explain: The sun rises in the East, so in the morning sunlight travels from East to West. A person's shadow is cast on the side opposite to the sun, meaning the shadow falls toward the West.
keywords.example: If you face North in the morning, sunlight hits your left side and your shadow extends to your right (West).

keywords.explain: If the shadow falls to the person's right, and shadows fall westward in the morning, then the person's right side faces West. This means the person is facing North.
keywords.example: Stand facing North: your right hand points East. But sun is in the East, so shadow falls West = to your left. For shadow on the right, you face South.

keywords.explain: Ms. X's back is to the building, so she is facing away from the building's front. The front door opens outward, so she just exited and now faces opposite to the building's front.
keywords.example: If the building faces South, its front door faces South. Ms. X exits and faces South (away from the building interior).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 280' width='320' height='280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing building facing South with Ms. X standing outside facing South, morning sun from East casting shadow to her right (West side).

given.aim: Determine which direction the front of the building faces, given that Ms. X's shadow falls to her right when she exits through the front door in the morning.

terms.meaning: Sun rises in the East; sunlight travels from East, so shadows are cast toward the West in the morning.
terms.example: A pole in the morning casts its shadow pointing westward.
terms.connects: This fixes the shadow direction as westward regardless of which way a person faces.

terms.meaning: The shadow falls on the right side of Ms. X, meaning West is to her right.
terms.example: If West is to your right, you are facing South (N is ahead, S is behind, E is left, W is right when facing South).
terms.connects: This tells us Ms. X is facing South after exiting the building.

terms.meaning: Ms. X exited through the front door, so her back is to the front of the building. She faces away from the front door.
terms.example: If Ms. X faces South, the front door (and building front) is behind her, meaning the building front faces South.
terms.connects: Since she faces South with her back to the building, the building's front door also faces South.

given.plan: 1. Morning sun is in the East, so shadows point West. 2. Shadow falls to Ms. X's right, so West is to her right. 3. If West is to your right, you face South. 4. She has her back to the front door, so she faces the same direction as the front of the building. 5. Therefore the building faces South.

steps.title: Establish shadow direction
steps.formula_raw: shadow direction = opposite to sun direction
steps.apply: Morning sun rises in the East. Shadows fall in the opposite direction = West. So Ms. X's shadow points West.
steps.note: This is a fixed cardinal fact: morning sun -> East, shadow -> West.

steps.title: Determine which way Ms. X faces
steps.formula_raw: right side faces West => person faces South
steps.apply: Shadow falls to Ms. X's right side, meaning West is to her right. When West is to the right and East is to the left, the person must be facing South (and North is behind).
steps.note: Use compass-hand rule: face South => left=East, right=West.

steps.title: Identify building front direction
steps.formula_raw: building front direction = direction Ms. X faces (since her back is to the door)
steps.apply: Ms. X exited and her back is to the building's front door. She faces South. Therefore the front door (and the building) faces South.
steps.note: The building front = the direction its door opens toward = South.

solution.result: The building is facing South. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this problem uses cardinal direction reasoning and the rule that shadows in the morning fall westward.

questions.question:
The front door of Mr. X's house faces East. Mr. X leaves the house, walking 50m straight from the back door that is situated directly opposite to the front door. He then turns to his right, walks for another 50m and stops. The direction of the point Mr. X is now located at with respect to the starting point is _______.

A. South-East  B. North-East  C. West  D. North-West

understand.plain: The front door faces East, so the back door faces West. Mr. X exits from the back door and walks 50m West. He then turns right from his westward direction and walks 50m more. We need to find the final direction from the starting point (back door).

keywords.explain: The back door is opposite to the front door. Since the front faces East, the back door faces West. Exiting the back door means walking West.
keywords.example: A house with front facing East has its back wall on the West side; the back door opens westward.

keywords.explain: When facing West, turning right (clockwise 90 degrees) makes you face North.
keywords.example: Face West: North is to your right. Turn right => now face North.

keywords.explain: After walking 50m West and then 50m North, the net displacement is 50m West and 50m North. The direction from the starting point to the final position is North-West.
keywords.example: Moving equal distances West and North places you at a 45-degree angle in the North-West quadrant.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 280' width='300' height='280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='280' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='150' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Mr. X Path from Back Door</text><text x='150' y='44' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>N</text><text x='150' y='270' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>S</text><text x='16' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>W</text><text x='284' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>E</text><rect x='150' y='130' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>House</text><text x='217' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>Front(E)</text><rect x='138' y='170' width='24' height='10' class='no'/><text x='110' y='179' text-anchor='end' class='sub no'>Back(W)</text><circle cx='150' cy='180' r='6' class='ok'/><text x='150' y='198' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Start</text><line x1='150' y1='180' x2='75' y2='180' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='112' y='174' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>50m W</text><circle cx='75' cy='180' r='5' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='75' y1='180' x2='75' y2='105' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='55' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>50m N</text><circle cx='75' cy='105' r='7' class='hi'/><text x='75' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>End</text><line x1='150' y1='180' x2='75' y2='105' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='96' y='152' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>NW</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Grid diagram showing Mr. X starting at the back door, walking 50m West, then 50m North, with the final position being North-West of the starting point.

given.aim: Find the compass direction of Mr. X's final position relative to his starting point (back door of the house).

terms.meaning: The front of the house and its front door face the East direction.
terms.example: Standing inside facing the front door means you face East.
terms.connects: This establishes the orientation of the entire house including the back door.

terms.meaning: The back door is on the wall directly opposite the front door, so it faces West.
terms.example: Front=East wall, Back=West wall; exiting back door means initially facing West.
terms.connects: Mr. X walks 50m in the West direction after exiting.

terms.meaning: Turning right (clockwise 90 degrees) from a westward heading results in facing North.
terms.example: W -> right turn -> N. Then walk 50m North.
terms.connects: The second leg of the journey is 50m northward.

given.plan: 1. Identify starting point = back door position. 2. Back door faces West: walk 50m West. 3. Turn right while facing West = now face North. 4. Walk 50m North. 5. Net displacement from start: 50m West + 50m North = North-West direction.

steps.title: Identify initial walking direction from back door
steps.formula_raw: back door direction = opposite of front door direction
steps.apply: Front door faces East => back door faces West. Mr. X exits back door and walks 50m heading West. Let start = (0, 0). After first leg: position = (-50, 0).

steps.title: Apply right turn from westward heading
steps.formula_raw: facing West + right turn (90 deg clockwise) = facing North
steps.apply: Mr. X was facing West. Turning right rotates 90 degrees clockwise: West -> North. He then walks 50m North. New position = (-50, 0) + (0, 50) = (-50, 50).
steps.note: Clockwise rotation: N->E->S->W->N. Reverse: W->N when turning right.

steps.title: Determine final direction from starting point
steps.formula_raw: direction = atan2(y, x) for displacement vector
steps.apply: Final position relative to start = (-50, 50). x is negative (West), y is positive (North). This places the point in the North-West quadrant. The angle is exactly 135 degrees from East, or 45 degrees West of North = North-West.
steps.note: Equal magnitude in West and North components confirms a pure 45-degree NW direction.

solution.result: The final position is North-West of the starting point. Answer: D. North-West.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formulas needed; the solution uses compass direction rules and coordinate tracking.

questions.question:
X is 1 km northeast of Y. Y is 1 km southeast of Z. W is 1 km west of Z. P is 1 km south of W. Q is 1 km east of P. What is the distance between X and Q in km?

A. 1  B. sqrt(2)  C. sqrt(3)  D. 2

understand.plain: Five points are defined using compass directions and unit distances. We place them on a coordinate grid starting from Z, compute each point's coordinates, then use the distance formula to find the separation between X and Q.

keywords.explain: Northeast is at 45 degrees above the East axis. Moving 1 km northeast means moving (1/sqrt(2)) km East and (1/sqrt(2)) km North.
keywords.example: If Y=(0,0), then X which is 1 km NE of Y = (1/sqrt(2), 1/sqrt(2)).

keywords.explain: Southeast is at 45 degrees below the East axis. Moving 1 km southeast means moving (1/sqrt(2)) km East and (1/sqrt(2)) km South.
keywords.example: Y is 1 km SE of Z: if Z=(0,0) then Y = (1/sqrt(2), -1/sqrt(2)).

keywords.explain: Distance between two points (x1,y1) and (x2,y2) is sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 300' width='340' height='300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='300' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='170' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Coordinate Map of Points</text><text x='170' y='44' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>N</text><text x='170' y='290' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>S</text><text x='16' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>W</text><text x='324' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>E</text><line x1='170' y1='55' x2='170' y2='285' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3,3'/><line x1='25' y1='170' x2='315' y2='170' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3,3'/><circle cx='110' cy='170' r='7' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='163' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Z</text><text x='110' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(0,0)</text><circle cx='60' cy='170' r='7' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='163' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>W</text><circle cx='60' cy='220' r='7' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P</text><circle cx='110' cy='220' r='7' class='ok'/><text x='110' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>Q</text><circle cx='175' cy='220' r='7' class='bg'/><text x='175' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Y</text><circle cx='240' cy='170' r='7' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='163' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>X</text><line x1='240' y1='170' x2='110' y2='220' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='185' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>sqrt(3)</text><text x='175' y='238' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>SE of Z</text><text x='240' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>NE of Y</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Coordinate map showing points Z at origin, W to the left, P below W, Q right of P (at same position as below Z), Y south-east of Z, X north-east of Y, with a dashed line between X and Q labeled sqrt(3).

given.aim: Compute the straight-line distance between points X and Q after placing all five points on a coordinate grid.

terms.meaning: Place Z at coordinates (0, 0) to anchor all other positions.
terms.connects: All other point positions are derived relative to Z.

terms.meaning: Y is 1 km southeast of Z: move (1/sqrt(2)) km East and (1/sqrt(2)) km South from Z.
terms.connects: X is computed relative to Y.

terms.term: Q derived from W and P
terms.meaning: W is 1 km west of Z => W=(-1,0). P is 1 km south of W => P=(-1,-1). Q is 1 km east of P => Q=(0,-1).
terms.connects: Q's final position is needed to compute distance XQ.

given.plan: 1. Set Z=(0,0). 2. Compute Y=(1/sqrt(2), -1/sqrt(2)). 3. Compute X = Y + NE offset = (sqrt(2), 0). 4. Compute W=(-1,0), P=(-1,-1), Q=(0,-1). 5. Apply distance formula to X and Q.

steps.title: Place all points on coordinate grid
steps.note: Northeast and Southeast unit vectors have magnitude 1 each, with equal East component and opposite vertical components.

steps.title: Apply distance formula between X and Q

solution.result: The distance between X and Q is sqrt(3) km. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: The 2D distance formula is the key tool; diagonal (NE/SE) movements decompose into equal horizontal and vertical components of magnitude 1/sqrt(2) each.

questions.question:
Mr. Vivek walks 6 meters North-east, then turns and walks 6 meters South-east, both at 60 degrees to east. He further moves 2 meters South and 4 meters West. What is the straight distance in meters between the point he started from and the point he finally reached?

A. 2*sqrt(2)  B. 2  C. sqrt(2)  D. 1/sqrt(2)

understand.plain: Mr. Vivek makes four movement segments. The phrase 'at 60 degrees to east' means both the NE and SE legs make a 60-degree angle with the East axis (not the standard 45-degree diagonal). We decompose each leg into East/North components using trigonometry, sum them all up, and compute the final straight-line distance.

keywords.explain: The NE direction here is at 60 degrees above the East axis, so its components are cos(60)=0.5 in the East direction and sin(60)=sqrt(3)/2 in the North direction.
SE at 60 degrees to east means 60 degrees below the East axis: East = cos(-60)=0.5, South = sin(60)=sqrt(3)/2 downward.
keywords.example: 6m at 60 deg SE: East = 3m, South = 3*sqrt(3)m (i.e., North = -3*sqrt(3)m).

keywords.explain: Sum all East-West and North-South components separately. The net vector gives the straight-line distance via the distance formula.
keywords.example: After NE + SE legs, East components add to 6m total, North components cancel to 0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 300' width='340' height='300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='300' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='170' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Vivek Path (scaled)</text><text x='170' y='44' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>N</text><text x='170' y='290' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>S</text><text x='16' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>W</text><text x='324' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>E</text><circle cx='80' cy='165' r='7' class='ok'/><text x='80' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Start</text><line x1='80' y1='165' x2='115' y2='100' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='85' y='128' text-anchor='end' class='sub mut'>NE 6m</text><text x='113' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>60 deg</text><line x1='115' y1='100' x2='150' y2='165' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='148' y='128' text-anchor='start' class='sub mut'>SE 6m</text><line x1='150' y1='165' x2='150' y2='190' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='162' y='182' text-anchor='start' class='sub mut'>S 2m</text><line x1='150' y1='190' x2='110' y2='190' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='130' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>W 4m</text><circle cx='110' cy='190' r='7' class='no'/><text x='110' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>End</text><line x1='80' y1='165' x2='110' y2='190' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='80' y='186' text-anchor='start' class='sub no'>2sqrt(2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Path diagram showing Vivek starting at left, going NE 6m (60 deg above east), then SE 6m (60 deg below east) returning to same height, then 2m South and 4m West, with red dashed line showing straight-line distance of 2*sqrt(2) from start.

given.aim: Find the total straight-line displacement from start to finish after four movement segments.

terms.connects: cos(60)=1/2, sin(60)=sqrt(3)/2 are the key trig values.

terms.meaning: East component = 6*cos(60) = 3m. South component = 6*sin(60) = 3*sqrt(3)m, so North = -3*sqrt(3)m.
terms.connects: The North-South components of NE and SE legs at the same angle cancel exactly.

terms.meaning: After all four legs, the net East-West and North-South displacements determine the final position.
terms.example: Start(0,0) -> after NE+SE: (6,0) -> after 2m South: (6,-2) -> after 4m West: (2,-2). Distance = sqrt(4+4) = 2*sqrt(2).
terms.connects: Only the last two legs produce net displacement.

given.plan: 1. Decompose NE leg: East=3, North=3*sqrt(3). 2. Decompose SE leg: East=3, North=-3*sqrt(3). 3. Sum all legs: East=3+3+0-4=2, North=3*sqrt(3)-3*sqrt(3)-2+0=-2. 4. Distance = sqrt(2^2 + (-2)^2) = sqrt(8) = 2*sqrt(2).

steps.title: Decompose NE and SE segments
steps.apply: NE leg (60 deg above East): East = 6*cos(60) = 6*(1/2) = 3m, North = 6*sin(60) = 6*(sqrt(3)/2) = 3*sqrt(3)m. SE leg (60 deg below East): East = 6*cos(60) = 3m, North = -6*sin(60) = -3*sqrt(3)m.
steps.note: The two vertical components are equal and opposite, so they cancel. Net after both legs: East=6m, North=0m. Position = (6, 0).

steps.title: Add remaining legs
steps.formula_raw: Net = sum of all displacement components
steps.apply: From (6, 0): move 2m South => (6, -2). Then move 4m West => (6-4, -2) = (2, -2). Final position = (2, -2).
steps.note: West is negative East direction; South is negative North direction.

steps.title: Compute straight-line distance from start
steps.apply: Start = (0, 0), End = (2, -2). d = sqrt(2^2 + (-2)^2) = sqrt(4 + 4) = sqrt(8) = 2*sqrt(2) meters.

solution.result: The straight-line distance is 2*sqrt(2) meters. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Trigonometric decomposition (cos/sin) is used for angled segments, followed by the 2D distance formula for the final net displacement.

questions.question:
M and N start from the same location. M travels 10 km East and then 10 km North-East. N travels 5 km South and then 4 km South-East. What is the shortest distance (in km) between M and N at the end of their travel?

A. 18.00  B. 22.50  C. 20.61  D. 25.00

understand.plain: Two people start from the same point. M goes 10 km East then 10 km NE (standard 45-degree diagonal). N goes 5 km South then 4 km SE (standard 45-degree diagonal). Both NE and SE here are at the standard 45 degrees. We find each person's final coordinates and compute the straight-line distance between them.

keywords.explain: Standard Northeast means 45 degrees from East. Components: East = 1/sqrt(2), North = 1/sqrt(2) per unit distance.
keywords.example: 10 km NE: East = 10/sqrt(2) ~ 7.071 km, North = 10/sqrt(2) ~ 7.071 km.

keywords.explain: Standard Southeast means 45 degrees below East. Components: East = 1/sqrt(2), South = 1/sqrt(2) per unit distance.
keywords.example: 4 km SE: East = 4/sqrt(2) ~ 2.828 km, South = 4/sqrt(2) ~ 2.828 km.

keywords.explain: After finding both final coordinates, use d = sqrt((Mx-Nx)^2 + (My-Ny)^2) to get the straight-line distance.

understand.visual_alt: Coordinate diagram showing M traveling 10km East then 10km NE ending at upper-right, N traveling 5km South then 4km SE ending at lower-right, with a dashed line between them labeled approximately 20.61 km.

given.aim: Find the straight-line distance between M and N after both complete their respective journeys from the same starting point.

terms.meaning: 10 km East: position (10, 0). Then 10 km NE (45 deg): add (10/sqrt(2), 10/sqrt(2)) = (7.071, 7.071). Final M = (17.071, 7.071).
terms.connects: NE is standard 45 degrees here, giving equal x and y increments.

terms.meaning: 5 km South: position (0, -5). Then 4 km SE (45 deg): add (4/sqrt(2), -4/sqrt(2)) = (2.828, -2.828). Final N = (2.828, -7.828).
terms.connects: SE is standard 45 degrees, giving equal positive x and negative y increments.

given.plan: 1. Set start = (0, 0). 2. Compute M = (10 + 10/sqrt(2), 10/sqrt(2)). 3. Compute N = (4/sqrt(2), -5 - 4/sqrt(2)). 4. Apply distance formula to M and N.

steps.title: Find N's final coordinates
steps.note: SE means positive East and negative North (southward).

steps.title: Compute distance between M and N

solution.result: The shortest distance between M and N is approximately 20.61 km. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: The 2D Euclidean distance formula connects all coordinate-based direction problems; diagonal (NE/SE) movements decompose into equal horizontal and vertical components using 1/sqrt(2) factor.
